narrative: As a Black male applicant with a middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on profiles of past students. The model estimated a 49.9% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which is high enough to tip the decision toward rejection, as the school prioritizes admitting those predicted to succeed strongly. This prediction was driven primarily by three key factors from your academic record: your LSAT score, your first-year school decile ranking, and your undergraduate GPA. Among these, your LSAT score of 35—below the typical 36.7 average—had the strongest positive influence, but in a favorable way, as it pushed the model's thinking toward you passing rather than failing. In contrast, your first-year decile of 4, lower than the average of about 5.7, was the top factor pulling toward a failure prediction, signaling to the model weaker early performance compared to most peers. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.6, well below the 3.21 average, ranked third in influence and also nudged the prediction toward failure, reinforcing concerns about your overall academic strength relative to typical admitted students. While being a full-time student aligns with the vast majority (over 90%) who succeed, it wasn't a major swing factor here. Overall, your profile showed solid potential in your LSAT but was held back by below-average early grades and undergrad performance, placing you in a riskier category than the stronger academic baselines the model favors. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man from a middle-income background—many overcome similar starts—but the model weighs these metrics heavily to minimize bar failure risks for the program. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it further, strengthening your undergrad record through post-bac courses or a master's, or targeting schools with more holistic reviews that value your unique story. Building a standout personal statement highlighting resilience could also help reframe these numbers in future applications. With targeted improvements, you can shift future predictions in your favor and pursue your legal ambitions.
